algorithms.name: Backtracking
algorithms.analogy: Solving a maze by trial and error — walk forward until you hit a wall, then back up to the last fork and try a different direction.
algorithms.story: A Sudoku solver: you place a digit in an empty cell and recurse. If you reach a contradiction, you erase (backtrack) and try the next digit. Without pruning, you'd try 9^81 combinations. With constraint propagation, you prune branches early — most real Sudoku boards solve in milliseconds.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Generate all permutations / combinations / subsets
• Constraint satisfaction (Sudoku, N-Queens, crossword)
• Find ALL solutions (not just one optimal)
• Search problems where state space is a tree of choices
• When greedy/DP fail because the problem requires exploring all paths
algorithms.howItWorks:
Template:
1. If current state is a valid solution → add to results
2. For each possible next choice:
3.   If choice is valid (constraint check):
4.     Make the choice (modify state)
5.     Recurse
6.     Undo the choice (backtrack — restore state)

Pruning makes this efficient:
• Check constraints BEFORE recursing (prune early)
• Sort candidates so identical choices are grouped (deduplication)
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Template: choose → explore → unchoose (the three steps are always present)
• Prune early: put the constraint check BEFORE the recursive call, not after
• For subsets with duplicates: sort first, then skip if nums[i] == nums[i-1] and i > start
• For permutations with duplicates: sort first, skip if used[i-1] is false and nums[i] == nums[i-1]
• N-Queens: track columns + two diagonal sets — checking constraints is O(1) per choice
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Sudoku Solver (Norvig's)","desc":"Peter Norvig's Python Sudoku solver uses backtracking with constraint propagation — solves any valid Sudoku in under a second."},{"title":"Regex Matching","desc":"Regex engines use backtracking to try different ways to match wildcards (*, ?) — leading to catastrophic backtracking with pathological inputs."},{"title":"Compiler Parser","desc":"Recursive descent parsers use backtracking to try different grammar rules when one fails — the 'try and rollback' pattern."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Not making a deep copy when adding to results: result.add(current) adds a reference — current changes later. Use new ArrayList<>(current)","Pruning too late: checking constraints after recursing (not before) misses the optimization entirely","Deduplication: when input has duplicates, you must sort + skip duplicate choices at the same level to avoid duplicate results","Exponential blowup without pruning: pure backtracking is O(n!) — always look for constraint checks to prune early"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Branch and Bound","desc":"Backtracking + upper/lower bound estimates to prune more aggressively — used in integer linear programming"},{"name":"Constraint Propagation","desc":"Arc consistency / forward checking — after each choice, propagate consequences to prune remaining choices"}]
algorithms.mermaidDiagram:
graph TD
    A["Start: empty path []"] --> B["Choose nums[0]=1"]
    A --> C["Choose nums[0]=2"]
    A --> D["Choose nums[0]=3"]
    B --> B1["[1,2] → add"]
    B --> B2["[1,3] → add"]
    C --> C1["[2,1] → add"]
    C --> C2["[2,3] → add"]
    D --> D1["[3,1] → add"]
    D --> D2["[3,2] → add"]
    B1 -.->|"unchoose"| A
    C1 -.->|"unchoose"| A

algorithms.name: Greedy Algorithms
algorithms.tags: ["O(n log n) typical", "Locally Optimal", "Activity Selection", "Interval Scheduling"]
algorithms.spaceComplexity: O(1) to O(n) · depends on problem
algorithms.analogy: Always eat the ripest fruit first — the locally optimal choice (ripest now) leads to the globally optimal outcome (least waste).
algorithms.story: You have meetings from 9am–5pm. Meetings overlap. Which ones do you attend to maximize count? Greedy insight: always pick the meeting that ends earliest. Proof: any other choice can only lead to fewer or equal meetings. Greedy works when local optimality implies global optimality — this is the key question to verify.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Interval scheduling / activity selection
• Huffman coding (optimal prefix-free encoding)
• Fractional Knapsack (NOT 0/1 Knapsack)
• Coin change with canonical coin systems
• Jump Game (can you reach the end?)
• Gas Station (circular route problem)
• Greedy works when: Exchange Argument proves local optimal → global optimal
algorithms.howItWorks:
Interval Scheduling (Activity Selection):
1. Sort intervals by END TIME
2. Select first interval
3. For each remaining interval:
4.   If interval start >= last selected end → select it
5.   Otherwise skip (it overlaps)
6. Result: maximum non-overlapping intervals

Proof: any solution that picks a later-ending activity can always swap it for the earlier-ending one without reducing the count.
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Always justify WHY greedy works — use exchange argument: 'if we swap X for Y, the solution can only worsen'
• Interval problems: sort by end time for max activities; sort by start time for merge intervals
• Jump Game: track farthest reachable index — greedy O(n) vs DP O(n²)
• Coin change greedy ONLY works for canonical coin systems (like real currency) — for arbitrary coins, use DP
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Huffman Coding (Compression)","desc":"ZIP/GZIP use Huffman trees — greedily merge the two least-frequent symbols to build the optimal prefix-free code."},{"title":"Kruskal's MST","desc":"Sort edges by weight, greedily add each if it doesn't create a cycle (Union-Find check) — produces the minimum spanning tree."},{"title":"Dijkstra's Algorithm","desc":"At each step, greedily commit to the node with the shortest known distance — combines greedy choice with priority queue."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Applying greedy when DP is needed — coin change with arbitrary coins requires DP, not greedy","Wrong sort criterion: activity selection sorts by END time, not start time — confusing these gives wrong answers","Not proving correctness — greedy algorithms are subtle; always verify with a proof or a counterexample search","Greedy for 0/1 Knapsack — taking highest value-per-weight doesn't work when items can't be split"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Interval Merging","desc":"Sort by start time, merge overlapping intervals — O(n log n) for grouping meeting rooms, calendar events"},{"name":"Task Scheduler (Cooldown)","desc":"Greedy with frequency counting — always schedule the most frequent remaining task to minimize idle time"}]
algorithms.mermaidDiagram:
graph TD
    A["Intervals: (1,3),(2,4),(3,5),(4,6)"] --> B["Sort by END time"]
    B --> C["Sorted: (1,3),(2,4),(3,5),(4,6)"]
    C --> D["Take (1,3). lastEnd=3"]
    D --> E{"(2,4): start=2 < lastEnd=3?"}
    E -- Yes,overlap --> F["Skip (2,4)"]
    F --> G{"(3,5): start=3 >= lastEnd=3?"}
    G -- Yes --> H["Take (3,5). lastEnd=5"]
    H --> I{"(4,6): start=4 < lastEnd=5?"}
    I -- Yes,overlap --> J["Skip (4,6)"]
    J --> K["Result: 2 activities"]
